coredata.dc:description: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) is a by-product of cell metabolism and is essential for cell signal transduction and physiological functions. However, excessive accumulation of ROS can lead to oxidative stress, causing cell damage and triggering diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and inflammatory bowel disease. Nanozymes, as emerging biocatalysts, show great potential in treating ROS-related conditions due to their ability to continuously scavenge ROS, their tunable size and surface properties, and their capability to mimic natural enzyme activity. The catalytic performance of nanozymes is influenced by their atomic structure. Various types of nanozymes, including glutathione peroxidase (GPx), oxidase (OXD), peroxidase (POD), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase (CAT), exhibit different catalytic activities and have been widely applied in biological detection, disease diagnosis, and therapeutic interventions. This review summarizes recent advancements in nanozyme research, particularly in the treatment of ROS-related diseases, exploring their types, antioxidant mechanisms, and applications in managing Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, osteoarthritis, ischemic stroke, rheumatoid arthritis, and inflammatory enteritis.

head.ce:title: Regulator of oxidative balance: Research progress of nanozymes in ROS-related diseases

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) is a by-product of cell metabolism and is essential for cell signal transduction and physiological functions. However, excessive accumulation of ROS can lead to oxidative stress, causing cell damage and triggering diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and inflammatory bowel disease. Nanozymes, as emerging biocatalysts, show great potential in treating ROS-related conditions due to their ability to continuously scavenge ROS, their tunable size and surface properties, and their capability to mimic natural enzyme activity. The catalytic performance of nanozymes is influenced by their atomic structure. Various types of nanozymes, including glutathione peroxidase (GPx), oxidase (OXD), peroxidase (POD), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase (CAT), exhibit different catalytic activities and have been widely applied in biological detection, disease diagnosis, and therapeutic interventions. This review summarizes recent advancements in nanozyme research, particularly in the treatment of ROS-related diseases, exploring their types, antioxidant mechanisms, and applications in managing Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, osteoarthritis, ischemic stroke, rheumatoid arthritis, and inflammatory enteritis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are a group of chemical substances produced during normal cellular metabolism. The ROS family includes superoxide anion (O2 ·⁻), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radical (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), peroxyl radical (LOO·), lipid hydroperoxides (LOOH), hypochlorous acid (HClO), and ozone (O3) [1]. In vivo, moderate levels of reactive oxygen species are essential for maintaining cell signal transduction and physiological functions. However, when the production of reactive oxygen species exceeds the antioxidant capacity of the cell, the excess ROS will react with various biological molecules within the cell, triggering oxidative stress, which causes damage to the cell [2]. Mild oxidative damage can lead to change in cellular function and behavior, such as accelerated aging, abnormal proliferation, and inflammatory responses. Severe oxidative damage, however, may result in apoptosis and autophagy.

In fact, the onset and progression of many diseases, including Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), wound healing, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), osteoarthritis (OA), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), and ischemic stroke (IS), are closely related to excessive ROS accumulation [3]. Under normal physiological conditions, the intracellular antioxidant system effectively removes excess ROS and maintains cellular homeostasis. However, under abnormal conditions, a significant increase in cellular ROS levels can impair the antioxidant system, leading to a severe imbalance in cellular antioxidant levels [4]. Therefore, maintaining redox homeostasis is essential for preventing ROS-dependent diseases, such as cancer, inflammation, radiation damage, and neurological disorders. Nanozymes, a novel class of biocatalysts with ROS scavenging capabilities, have demonstrated significant potential in treating ROS-related disorders. Unlike traditional small-molecule antioxidants, nanozymes continuously remove excess ROS through catalytic reactions, effectively alleviating oxidative stress and modulating inflammatory responses.

This sustained clearance ability enhances the stability and durability of nanozymes' therapeutic effects. Additionally, nanozymes offer the advantage of size controllability and ease of surface modification, which provide enhanced regulatory options for their distribution, targeting, and biocompatibility within organisms [5]. As nanomaterials that mimic the activity of natural enzymes, nanozymes address the limitations of natural enzymes, such as high cost, production complexity, stability issues, and environmental sensitivity [6]. The atomic structure of nanozymes is a key factor determining their catalytic activity [7]. Based on their catalytic functions, nanozymes can be categorized into several types, including glutathione peroxidase (GPx), glucose oxidase (GOD), oxidase (OXD) [8], peroxidase (POD) [9], superoxide dismutase (SOD) [10], and catalase (CAT) [11]. These nanozymes play a crucial role in various fields, including immunoassays, disease diagnosis, and treatment, thanks to their diverse enzyme activities. Notably, nanozymes have demonstrated significant effectiveness in eliminating excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) accumulated in the body. This capability has garnered considerable attention for treating ROS-related disorders, such as wound healing, osteoarthritis (OA), and Parkinson's disease (PD) [12]. Additionally, when nanozymes and drug molecules (small molecule drugs, nucleic acids and proteins) form hybrid materials of nanozyme hydrogels and apply them to targeted therapy, the distribution of therapeutic drugs can be significantly improved. This hybrid material not only has the unique catalytic properties of nanozymes, but also can use the good biocompatibility and drug loading capacity of hydrogels to accurately deliver drug molecules to the lesion site. In this way, the effect of the drug on the non-target site can be minimized, that is, the off-target effect, thereby reducing systemic toxicity [13,14].

Previous studies of nanozymes may have focused more on specific catalytic mechanisms, but today, the application of nanozymes has gone far beyond this category, especially in the field of antioxidant disease treatment, showing great potential [15]. Nanozymes can effectively slow down or even prevent the occurrence of pathological changes by efficiently removing reactive oxygen species in the body and reducing oxidative stress. This new form of treatment can not only effectively intervene in the early stage of the disease and inhibit its progression, but also provide protective preventive measures for the disease, rather than reactive treatment in the traditional sense. This means that nanozymes can build a strong defense line for the disease when it has not yet fully erupted. Through in-depth study of the role of nanozymes in disease modification, we can not only develop more accurate treatment methods, but also bring more effective and lasting treatment effects to many patients.

In this review, we systematically summarize recent research on nanozymes and their role in diseases related to ROS dysregulation. We first introduce the various types of nanozymes and their antioxidant mechanisms. Next, we provide a detailed overview of how nanozymes treat Parkinson's disease (PD), Alzheimer's disease (AD), osteoarthritis (OA), ischemic stroke (IS), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), and inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) by scavenging ROS and regulating oxidative stress. This discussion aims to help researchers understand both current findings and future research directions. Finally, we explore the potential clinical applications of nanozymes, hoping that this review will serve as a valuable reference for future basic research and clinical advancements involving nanozymes (Scheme 1 ).

Enzymes are biocatalysts essential for most chemical reactions in living systems, regulating the balance of oxidation processes within the body [16]. However, the natural abundance of enzymes in organisms is limited, and their catalytic activity is significantly influenced by external environmental conditions [17]. As a result, these enzymes often struggle to function effectively under abnormal physiological conditions. With the advancement of nanotechnology, a new class of artificial enzymes, known as nanozymes, has been developed. Nanozymes not only address the inherent limitations of natural enzymes but also offer superior catalytic performance, making them promising substitutes. Nanozymes, as emerging stars in the field of scientific research, have garnered significant attention for their exceptional catalytic efficiency, stability, and biocompatibility. In particular, inorganic nanozymes [18] have become a focal point of research due to their straightforward synthesis processes and outstanding catalytic activity. Organic nanozymes [19], with their unique molecular structures and ease of modification, exhibit tremendous potential in the fields of biomedicine and environmental remediation. Composite nanozymes, such as metal-organic framework (MOFs) nanozymes [20], demonstrate superior catalytic performance and selectivity due to their highly ordered porous structures and tunable active sites. Compared to single nanozymes, hybrid nanozymes typically display enhanced catalytic efficiency and stability, as well as a broader range of applications. These diverse nanozymes not only expand the variety of nanozymes but also provide a richer array of options and a wider scope for research and application across various fields (Scheme 2 ).

Inorganic nanozymes can be categorized into metal-based and non-metal-based types [16]. Metal-based nanozymes include materials such as gold (Au) [21–23], platinum (Pt) [24], palladium (Pd) [25], iridium (Ir) [26], cerium oxide (CeO2) [27], manganese oxide (MnO2) [28], cobalt oxide (Co3O4) [29], and silver-platinum (Ag@Pt) [30] composite nanozymes. Among them, Pt nanozyme, as a rising star, has high catalytic efficiency and multi-enzyme function in different physical/chemical environments [31]. Yang et al. designed a multi-metal composite Pt/Co-SA-NSG nanozyme. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations showed that the synergistic effect of Co single atoms and Pt NPs increased the adsorption energy of free radicals and reduced the activation energy of the reaction. Co and Pt act as independent active centers, which jointly promote the enzyme mimic activity, increase the SOD and CAT enzyme mimic activity, effectively remove ROS, and treat OA [32].

These nanozymes are known for their clear chemical structure, stability, and controllability. On the other hand, non-metal-based nanozymes include carbon-based nanozymes such as fullerenes, carbides, nitrides, and carbon nanodots. These carbon-based nanozymes exhibit diverse enzyme activities due to their unique electronic and geometric structures. It is worth noting that compared with natural enzymes or traditional mimic enzymes, inorganic nanozymes have the advantages of easy preparation, low cost and high stability [33]. Furthermore, the excellent physical and chemical properties of inorganic nanozymes (such as optical, electrical, magnetic, thermal and mechanical properties, as well as large specific surface area) not only endow them with dual or even multiple functions, but also provide the possibility for the effective regulation of their catalytic activity and the construction of probes for surface modification [34]. However, most of the bare noble metal nanozymes (except Au) have biological toxicity, which limits their application in clinical medicine. This biotoxicity problem makes the application of these nanozymes in the human body full of risks and uncertainties, which may cause damage to cells and affect organ function [35]. Existing safety studies mainly cover limited basic toxicology, which are not sufficient for a comprehensive and in-depth assessment. The scope of toxicological research needs to be expanded to include effects on different biological systems, potential risks of long-term exposure, and possible detoxification mechanisms. The development of new biocompatible inorganic nanozymes is still an urgent problem to be solved. Only through systematic and comprehensive research can we promote the wide application of inorganic nanozymes in clinical medicine and truly realize their potential in disease diagnosis and treatment.

Composite nanozymes, including metal organic composite nanozymes and metal oxide composite nanozymes. These mixed nanozymes typically have better catalytic efficiency and stability, making them suitable for fields involving multiple catalytic reactions. Among them, porous hybrid MOF nanozymes composed of metal ions or metal clusters combined with organic ligands have become a research hotspot due to their unique porous structure and functional diversity. Prussian blue (PB) and its derivatives are notable examples of MOF-based nanozymes [36]. These materials not only exhibit significant antioxidant activity but are also favored for their excellent biocompatibility and biosafety. Huang et al. reported a folic acid (FA) -modified MOF, using its porous structure to load baicalin (Bai) to synthesize a macrophage-targeted delivery system Bai@FA-UIO-66-NH2 nanoplatform. The platform can alleviate the inflammatory response by scavenging ROS and regulating the polarization of M1 to M2 macrophages to achieve effective OA treatment [37]. The advantages of MOF-based nanozymes include their adjustable structural characteristics, abundant functional groups, large specific surface area, and excellent thermal stability. These features provide broad prospects for their applications in biomedicine and catalysis [38]. However, further development of MOF-based nanozymes faces several challenges. These include the inherent limitations of framework materials, the accuracy of doping techniques, the need for a deeper understanding of the relationship between catalytic activity and structure, and the limitations of characterization techniques [39].

Organic nanozymes are nanomaterials formed through the self-assembly and processing of small organic molecules or polymers. They include materials such as hemoglobin [40], metal porphyrins [41], DNA complexes [42], and various organic compounds [43]. At the same time, organic metals can be anchored on inorganic carriers by covalent or non-covalent bonds to obtain inorganic/organic hybrid nanomaterials, and the intrinsic enzyme-like activity of these nanozymes is completely derived from the organic metal monolayer, rather than the inorganic carrier [44]. For example, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) are the most commonly used inorganic carriers because they are easy to prepare and surface functionalized with thiol ligands. Organic nanozymes can effectively regulate their functions by precisely controlling their electronic properties due to their uniform distribution of catalytically active sites. This precise control makes organic nanozymes have broad application prospects in many fields, especially in biomedicine and free radical scavenging. The manufacturing process of organic nanozymes differs significantly from that of inorganic nanozymes, primarily due to the weaker interactions between organic nanoparticles. This weakness makes them prone to merging or aggregating into larger particles under dynamic conditions. Introducing cofactors is an effective strategy to prevent such aggregation and enhance their antioxidant efficiency [19], Despite these advancements, the relationship between the physical and chemical properties of organic nanozymes and their catalytic behavior remains complex. This complexity leads to a limited understanding of their catalytic mechanisms and kinetics. Currently, theoretical research on organic nanozymes is still developing, and there is an urgent need to devise new methods to address these inherent complexities. Such advancements are crucial for a more comprehensive understanding and utilization of the unique properties of these materials.

The body's defense system against oxidative stress relies on two main mechanisms: enzymatic defense and non-enzymatic defense. The core of the enzymatic defense system comprises four key antioxidant enzymes: CAT, SOD, GPx, and POD (Scheme 3 ). Additionally, enzymes such as OXD and GOD contribute to maintaining oxidative balance and stabilizing the intracellular environment. In contrast, the non-enzymatic defense system counteracts oxidative stress through antioxidants such as vitamin E, melanin, glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid, and other tripeptides that react with and neutralize ROS, these antioxidants form a protective line of defense within the cell to shield it from oxidative damage [45]. As an artificial alternative enzyme, nanozymes are widely used in various fields. Accurately estimating the catalytic activity of nanozymes under actual conditions, by selectively supplementing exogenous antioxidant enzymes, nanozymes can more effectively assist cells to resist oxidative stress, thereby maintaining cell health and function [46].

CAT is an enzyme widely found in organisms, responsible for catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2. With the discovery of CAT-like activity in Fe2O3 nanoparticles, scientists have identified over 100 types of CAT nanozymes, including those based on noble metals such as gold (Au), platinum (Pt), silver (Ag), palladium (Pd), and iridium (Ir) [47], as well as metal oxides like cerium (Ce), iron (Fe), manganese (Mn), ruthenium (Ru), copper (Cu), and molybdenum (Mo) [48], as well as sulfides and carbonitrides. For example, in terms of metal oxide nanozymes, Celardo et al. proposed a catalytic model of cerium oxide nanozymes with catalytic properties. H2O2 can oxidize CeO2 from Ce3+ to Ce4+, which is a reversible oxidation. After the complete degradation of H2O2, it remains persistent in aqueous solution for a long time and is reduced through an unclear mechanism [49]. However, it has recently been found that nano-cerium dioxide can react with H2O2 in another way, which has very important implications in terms of scavenging free radicals in the body [50]. In this system, there is a small amount of 2Ce4+ binding site presented by the oxygen vacancy site of H2O2 and CeO2. Then, with the release of the proton, two electrons are transferred to the two Ce4+, forming a completely reduced oxygen vacancy site. Oxygen is produced from the reduced oxygen vacancy site, and then, another H2O2 molecule is bound to the 2Ce3+ site. The homolysis of the O–O bond occurs during the transfer of two electrons and the absorption of two protons. This mechanism involves the reaction of H2O2 with Ce4+, where Ce4+ is reduced to Ce3+, while H2O2 is oxidized to the molecule O2 through A catalase-like reaction, and the low Ce3+/Ce4+ ratio facilitates this process (Fig. 1 A) [51]. Wang et al. described in detail the catalytic cycle corresponding to the CAT simulated activity of OV-free CeO2 (111) (Fig. 1B). Reaction (i) is the oxidation of an H2O2 molecule by the CeO2 (111) surface to produce a molecule O2 and a reduced H2–CeO2 (111) surface; (ii) is a subsequent reaction between H2–CeO2 (111) and another H2O2 molecule, producing two water molecules and completing the catalytic cycle. This mechanism is significantly different from the CAT simulated activity of precious metals, which can only occur with the help of pre-adsorbed OH groups. (i) The detailed mechanism and energy distribution of the reaction, which is the external energy (ΔE = −1.40eV), with a smaller energy barrier (Ea = 0.35 eV). This indicates that hydrogen peroxide is easily oxidized by CeO2 (111) to O2. And the mechanism and energy distribution of the reaction (ii), which is also thermodynamically and kinetically favorable (ΔE = −2.09eV, Ea = 0.82eV). These results indicate that CeO2 (111) has considerable CAT simulation activity [52]. These CAT-like nanozymes effectively mitigate ROS accumulation by catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2, thereby offering antioxidant protection to cells and counteracting oxidative stress. Notably, this catalytic reaction involves the continuous interaction of two H2O2 molecules with the active sites of CAT, and the decomposition rate increases with higher H2O2 concentrations.

SOD is a key antioxidant enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of O2 ·⁻ into H2O2. Currently, over 70 nanozymes with SOD-like activity have been identified. These include metals and metal oxides such as Pt, Au, Cu, Mn, nickel (Ni), cobalt (Co), Mo, Rh, Fe, and vanadium (V), as well as carbides [53], nitrides, sulfides, and carbon-based materials like cerium oxide (CeO2) and fullerene [54], These SOD-like nanozymes effectively mitigate oxidative stress by removing excessive ROS, thereby protecting cells, delaying aging, and inhibiting inflammatory responses [55], During the catalytic process, SOD converts O2 ·⁻ produced in metabolism into H2O2, which can subsequently be decomposed by CAT. Consequently, many SOD-like nanozymes also exhibit CAT-like activity, providing a more comprehensive removal of ROS. Compared to natural SOD enzymes or other small molecule antioxidants, these nanozymes offer more pronounced advantages [56]. Zhong et al. designed a graphene-based Cl–Cu–N4 centered Highly-active single-atom nanozymes (Cu–N4ClG) for SOD-like reactions [57]. After precise manipulation of N and Cl atoms, the geometric and electronic structures of the Cl–Cu–N4 active center were verified by DFT calculations to match the natural SOD enzyme atoms. By further studying its antioxidant mechanism, the coupled electron transfer model proposed by Celardo et al. was once considered as a reasonable mechanism to explain the SOD simulation characteristics of cerium oxide NPs (Fig. 2 A) [51]. H2O2 undergoes the oxidation half reaction (Fig. 2A, the same as Fig. 1A), and an O2 ·− molecule binds to the reduced oxygen vacancy site (Fig. 2A➄). Hydrogen peroxide is then released through the absorption of two protons and the electron transfer of one Ce3+ (Fig. 2A➅). By repeating this reaction with a second O2 ·− molecule, the original nano-cerium oxide state can be regenerated (Fig. 2A➆). Based on in-depth consideration of the real structure and electronic properties of cerium oxide, Wang et al. proposed a polishing catalytic cycle mechanism of nano-cerium oxide as a simulation of SOD [52]. The surface defect state is very important for enzyme-like activity. After co-adsorption of HO· 2 on the surface of cerium oxide, an intermediate is formed (Fig. 2B). The reaction between the intermediate and another HO· 2 free radical can then release hydrogen peroxide and O2, and the nano-cerium oxide returns to its initial state. This mechanism is consistent with that of the simulation activity of SOD in precious metals studied by DFT calculation. With the help of strict density functional theory and micro dynamic models, Guo et al. studied the Langmuir-Hinshelwood (LH) and Ely-Redier (ER) mechanisms respectively to describe the SOD-like activity of Co3O4 (Fig. 2C) [58]. The ER mechanism of Co3O4 is more feasible. Because the barrier involved through the ER mechanism is lower than the barrier involved through the LH mechanism. The O2 ·− molecule captures a proton from the water, forming OH− and HO2 ·. The ER mechanism begins with the chemisorption of HO2 · on the surface of Co3O4 to form an intermediate (INT1) with an adsorption energy of −35.52 kcal mol−1. Thereafter, INT1 reacts with a second HO2 · to release H2O2 and O2, accompanied by Co3O4 regeneration. The activation barrier of the basic reaction through the transition state (TS1) is 19.02 kcal mol−1.

GPx is a crucial enzyme that captures free radicals in the body. It uses glutathione (GSH) or thioredoxin (TRX) as electron donors to reduce organic and inorganic hydroperoxides to the corresponding alcohols, producing glutathione disulfide (GSSG) in the process. This reaction not only lowers intracellular peroxide levels but also regulates the redox state of cells, which is essential for maintaining intracellular stability. In research on nanozymes that mimic GPx activity, various nanomaterials have been developed to replicate the catalytic functions of natural GPx enzymes. Examples include selenium (Se), vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) [59], and selenium dioxide (SeO2) [60]. Selenium-based nanozymes, in particular, are considered ideal for simulating GPx activity due to their unique chemical properties and good biocompatibility. Nanoscale selenium can mimic the active selenium center in GPx, effectively removing peroxides. As a potent antioxidant enzyme, GPx exhibits widespread application in cell protection, anti-inflammation, neuroprotection, tumor treatment, and anti-aging. Through continuous in-depth research on its antioxidant mechanism, Flohe et al. discovered that GPx1 in bovine red blood cells operates via a ping-pong mechanism, wherein the substrate and product alternately bind to or release from the enzyme [61]. Further studies have demonstrated that the catalytic mechanisms of GPx1, GPx3, and GPx4 can all be represented by a catalytic cycle (Fig. 3 A). In SecGPxs, selenol (-SeH) reacts with H2O2 in the form of selenolate ions to generate selenenic acid (-SeOH), which is subsequently reduced back to -SeH by two molecules of GSH, forming GSSG and water. Specifically, the first GSH molecule forms a selenodisulfide bond with selenenic acid, releasing oxygen as water. The second GSH molecule reduces the selenodisulfide through a thiol-disulfide exchange, releasing GSSG and regenerating the enzyme in the selenol form, ready for the next catalytic cycle. Vernekar et al. introduced the catalytic cycle of vanadium pentoxide nanowires (Fig. 3B) [62]. The initial step involves the reaction between H2O2 and the active surface to generate the vanadium peroxide intermediate 1, which is then attacked by GSH to form the sulfonate-bound intermediate 2. Compared with natural GPx, intermediate 2 undergoes hydrolysis to produce glutathione sulfonic acid (3, GSOH) and a dihydroxy intermediate 4, which participates in a redox reaction with another H2O2 molecule to regenerate intermediate 1. Meanwhile, GSOH reacts with another GSH molecule to form glutathione disulfide (GSSG). In this catalytic process, the interaction between H2O2 and the active site VO to form vanadium peroxide is crucial. Ghosh et al. reported that V2O5 nanowires exhibit GPx-like activity in the presence of GSH, protecting cells from oxidative damage [59]. By analyzing its molecular mechanism, they elucidated how V2O5 can be converted into a cell-protective antioxidant in the presence of H2O2 and GSH (Fig. 3C).

POD is a ubiquitous and highly active enzyme found in many organisms. It catalyzes the oxidation of substrates, such as phenolic compounds and amines, by H2O2. POD plays a crucial role in various metabolic processes, including detoxification, cell signal transduction, and immune responses. It converts H2O2 into water while simultaneously oxidizing the substrates. POD-like activity is also a common characteristic of many transition metals and carbon-based nanozymes, including cobalt (Co), manganese (Mn), copper (Cu), iridium (Ir), iron (III) oxide (Fe2O3), ferrous sulfide, and Prussian blue. These POD-like nanozymes promote the reduction of H2O₂ to water by oxidizing substrates such as diazoaminobenzene (DAB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [15]. The catalytic mechanism of POD simulators based on nanomaterials is generally Fenton or Fenton reactions or electron transfer processes [63]. Wang et al. prepared Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MNPs) with significantly improved POD-like activity by reverse co-precipitation method [64], and proposed the possible catalytic mechanism of Fe3O4 nanoparticles as POD mimics to activate H2O2 (Fig. 4 A). H2O2 molecules are adsorbed on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, and then activated by the binding Fe2+ and Fe3+ to generate ·OH and O2 −·/HO· 2 free radicals. Due to the high oxidation capacity of ROS, the generated ·OH and O2 −·/HO· 2 radicals initiate the subsequent degradation and mineralization of Rhodamine B (RhB). However, Maxim et al. put forward different views on the formation of ·OH under the condition of bio-related superoxygen-driven Fenton reaction [65]. Based on spin capture electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments, they found that the POD simulation properties responsible for the NPs of γ-Fe2O3 and ferric oxide were not metal ions released by the nanoparticles (Fig. 4B). In addition, the catalytic center on the surface of γ-Fe2O3 has a production effect at least 50 times that of dissolved metal ions. In recent years, electron transfer related mechanisms have been applied to POD simulators, such as IrO2/rGO nanocomposites [66], FePt–Au hybrid NPs [67], Co3O4 NPs [68], and AuNPs@CD nanocomposites [69]. Cao et al. used FeS2(100) and Fe3S4(001) surface H2O2 oxidation TMB as a model, and studied its catalytic mechanism by DFT calculation and experimental characterization. The results showed that the catalytic activity of FeS2 nanozymes was significantly higher than that of Fe3S4 nanozymes [70]. The DFT calculation shows that the iron ions on the surface of the iron sulfide nanozymes can effectively catalyze the production of ·OH through the interaction between the 3d electrons of Fe and the leading orbital of H2O2 in the range of −10 to 5 eV. However, FeS2 nanozymes exhibit higher POD-like activity due to the fact that surface Fe (II) binds to H2O2 to form an inner orbital complex, resulting in a greater binding energy and a smaller H2O2 base decomposition energy barrier (Fig. 4C). In contrast, iron ions on the surface of Fe3S4 nanozymes bind to H2O2 to form an outer orbital complex, resulting in a smaller binding energy and a larger H2O2 base decomposition energy barrier. Charge transfer analysis showed that FeS2 and Fe3S4 nanozymes transferred 0.12e and 0.05e from surface iron ions to H2O2, respectively. Li et al. introduced copper ions into Au Hops by hydrothermal method to synthesize nano-enzymes (Au/Cu Hops). In the presence of H2O2, Pod-like activity of Au/Cu Hops was tested using 2,2′-azo-di-(3-ethylbenzothiazolin-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) as a probe (Fig. 4D). Moreover, the catalytic mechanism of Cu–S clusters in nanozymes simulating PPO and POD was also proposed by DFT calculation [71].

(Reproduced with permission [64]. Copyright 2009, Elsevier). (B) Mechanism mediated by γ-Fe2O3 NPs. (Reproduced with permission [65]. Copyright 2011, UNITED STATES). (C) The POD catalytic activity mechanism of FeS2 and Fe3S4 nanozymes (Reproduced with permission [70]. Copyright 2024, American Chemical Society). (D) The surface of Au/Cu Hops forms a double catalytic active site POD-like mimetic enzyme catalytic mechanism.(Reproduced with permission [71]. Copyright 2023, Science China Press).

GOD catalyzes the conversion of glucose into gluconic acid and H2O2 in the presence of O2, which not only regulates the hyperglycemia microenvironment, but also has antibacterial and oxygen supply functions, so it shows great potential in the treatment of diabetic wounds. By consuming glucose to starve bacterial cells to produce H2O2 for oxidative therapy [72]. Zhang et al. constructed a ZnS-GC nanozyme with good biocompatibility and GOx and CAT enzyme activities. When they encountered hyperglycemia and oxidative wound microenvironment at the wound site, the presence of GOx and CAT would be activated by glucose to perform an effective local cascade catalytic cycle reaction [73]. Yu et al. developed a glucose/ROS cascaded nanozyme (CHA@GOx) for the treatment of diabetic wounds based on Ce-driven dual ligands (alendronate and 2-methylimidazole) and GOx co-assembly [74]. It had SOD and CAT enzyme simulation activity, which could effectively remove excess ROS. In particular, it can catalyze the excessive H2O2 produced by glucose oxidation reaction to produce O2, regulate wound oxygen balance, and reduce the toxic and side effects of GOx, so as to achieve the purpose of synergistic repair of diabetic wounds. Bankar et al. used molecular oxygen as an electron acceptor to effectively catalyze the oxidation of β-d-glucose to gluconic acid and H2O2 [75]. This catalytic reaction consists of reduction steps and oxidation steps. Initially, in a reduction half-reaction, GOx catalyzes the oxidation of β-d-glucose to d-glucose-delta-lactone, which is hydrolyzed to gluconic acid, while the FAD of GOx is reduced to FADH2. In the oxidation half reaction, FADH2 is reoxidized by oxygen to form FAD and H2O2 (Fig. 5 A). Liao et al. explored the therapeutic potential of GOx for different diseases by studying the entire process of glucose catabolism [76]. For example, a prolonged high-glucose environment is detrimental to skin wound healing in a number of ways, such as the accumulation of advanced glycation end products, peripheral neuropathy, impaired extracellular matrix (ECM) deposition, impaired angiogenesis, and neutrophil - and macrophage-led immune regulatory imbalances. Through the catabolism of glucose in local tissues, GOx reduces local glucose levels, thereby helping to correct these adverse factors, with clear or potential therapeutic implications for diabetic wounds (Fig. 5B).

Oxidase catalyzes the oxidation of substrates (electron donors). In the presence of oxygen, oxidase directly catalyzes the substrate without the participation of harmful intermediates such as hydrogen peroxide, so the catalytic process is safer and more convenient [77]. The oxidase family is classified according to the functional groups of the donor, including amino groups, CH–OH group (GOx), Ph-OH group (polyphenol oxidase), sulfur groups (sulfate oxidase, SuOx) and ferrous ion (iron oxide enzyme and cytochrome C oxidase) [78]. To date, Co3O4 [79], Ce [80], FeNZ [81], Pt [82], and other materials have been found to have oxidase mimic activity. Chen et al. synthesized Fe SAs/N5-pC-4 with Fe–N5 active center by polymerization/pyrolysis/evaporation/etching, which had the same heme site coordination structure as natural cytochrome C oxidase, and revealed its potential enzyme-like mechanism through experiments and density functional theory calculations [83]. The formation and electron transfer processes of intermediates such as 1O2, O2 ·− have been shown to have important effects on the OXD properties of these nanozymes [84]. In the possible reaction mechanism of Mn3O4 NPs proposed by Zhang et al. (Fig. 6 A), electrons transferred from manganese to O2 lead to the formation of O2 ·−, which is partly responsible for the formation of H2O2 and O2 through non-enzymatic or SOD-catalyzed decomposition [85]. Then, part of the hydrogen peroxide produced reacts with dissolved Mn2+ to decompose into ·OH. Later, the intermediates ·OH/O2 ·− and Mn3+ oxidize TMB to form the TMB-Mn3O4 NP system. Bhattacharyya et al. synthesized a water-soluble Pd12 nanocage using a benzothiadiazol-based tetrapyridinyl donor L with a 90° cis-blocking Pd (II) receptor (Fig. 6B) through ligand-driven self-assembly to synthesize a polyhedral solid structure topology representing an unusual twisted cube geometry [86]. The special structure of the water-soluble discrete coordination structure of the nanocages showed excellent oxidase-like activity. The lower K m value indicates that the active center of Pd-cage has a high affinity for the substrate molecule TMB, and the higher K cat value indicates that the nanocages have a very high catalytic efficiency compared with various reported nanozymes. And the temperature increase helps to accelerate the non-radiative decay that directly competes with the photosensitization. Reduced photoactivation of oxygen leads to reduced oxidation of TMB. Although simulated oxidase nanozymes have been extensively studied, few specific biomolecules have been explored as substrates, especially ascorbate oxidase (AAO) simulated nanozymes. Here, He et al. demonstrated that copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs), as a catalyst, were oxidized by dissolved O2 as a green oxidizing agent to oxidize ascorbic acid (AA), thus forming dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA), by exploring ascorbate oxidase (AAO) simulation nanozymes, thus revealing a new OXD simulator [87]. Under neutral conditions, the Michaelis-Menten constant for CuO nanoparticles is 0.1302 mM, while that for AAO is 0.084 mM. Moreover, CuO nanoparticles exhibit greater robustness compared to AAO nanoparticles, making them suitable for applications in a diverse range of conditions (Fig. 6C). To verify this, a fluorescent AA sensor was developed based on the simulative activity of CuO NPs towards AAO. The DHAA reaction, catalyzed by the oxidation of AA with O-phenylenediamine (OPDA) and CuO NPs, was employed to obtain the fluorescent product.

The application of nanozymes in ROS-related diseases is progressing steadily. Current research primarily focuses on mimicking the activities of CAT, SOD, GPx, POD to eliminate ROS, regulate oxidative stress, and reduce inflammation to treat related diseases [88]. In practical applications, the rational design and optimization of nanozymes can significantly enhance their therapeutic efficacy in treating diseases associated with oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of nanozymes in ROS-related diseases
ce:section.ce:para: In biological systems, excessive ROS can cause oxidative damage to tissues. For instance, high concentrations of ROS can lead to carbonylation and subsequent protein denaturation [89]. Additionally, excessive ROS can result in lipid peroxidation or DNA damage [90]. Severe oxidative injuries can contribute to various diseases, including neurodegenerative disorders, stroke, osteoarthritis, and inflammatory enteritis. To prevent such damage, numerous antioxidant enzymes in organisms, such as SOD, CAT, and POD, work together to maintain cellular redox balance. However, due to insufficient endogenous enzyme levels, it is often necessary to introduce exogenous antioxidants to counteract oxidative stress-related diseases. Among artificial antioxidants, nanozyme-based antioxidants are gaining significant attention for their ability to mimic the functions of natural antioxidant enzymes. This chapter reviews the recent applications of nanozymes in the treatment of Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, osteoarthritis, ischemic stroke, rheumatoid arthritis, and inflammatory enteritis (Table 1 ).

AD is one of the most prevalent neurodegenerative disorders, characterized by memory impairment and cognitive decline, which can lead to abnormalities in language, behavior, visual-spatial orientation, and motor functions [148]. Studies suggest that the pathogenesis of AD is linked to several factors: the accumulation of misfolded β-amyloid (Aβ) peptide plaques in the brain, elevated levels of ROS, and neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein [149]. The accumulation of Aβ peptides triggers ROS production, which disrupts mitochondrial membrane potential and calcium (Ca2⁺) homeostasis [150]. Abnormal tau protein can bind to dynein-related protein 1 (DRP1), impairing mitochondrial transport and affecting energy transfer, ultimately leading to mitochondrial dysfunction [151]. The interaction between Aβ and tau protein is closely associated with oxidative stress, resulting in reduced activity of antioxidant enzymes such as GPx, SOD, CAT in the brains of AD patients, which contributes to antioxidant imbalance and apoptosis. In addressing these issues, nanozymes with antioxidant properties hold significant potential for the treatment of AD [152]. Nanozymes present a promising new strategy for treating AD by regulating ROS levels and Aβ protein deposition and oxidative stress.

Antioxidant nanozymes show significant potential in AD treatment by effectively penetrating the blood-brain barrier (BBB) [153]. Du et al. reported a 2D Nb2C nanozyme exhibiting strong antioxidant enzyme mimic activity, including SOD, POD and CAT activities [93]. The powerful antioxidant properties of this nanozyme can remove excess cellular ROS, and its internal mechanism is revealed by computational simulation. Notably, it can also chelate Cu2+ and reduce Aβ peptide deposition. Under near-infrared laser irradiation, Nb2C nanozymes enhance BBB permeability, overcoming the limitations of many conventional anti-AD treatment drugs. Huang et al. developed a multifunctional nanozyme-based therapy that combines efficient gene and drug delivery. Their approach, using PPAR-siSOX9, improves Aβ clearance and nerve regeneration in AD (Fig. 7 A) [154]. This therapy enhances neuronal differentiation by synergistically regulating the Wnt/β-catenin and retinoic acid (RA) signaling pathways using a siRNA expression plasmid, RA, and Ag2S quantum dots (QDs). The QDs’ fluorescence in the second near-infrared window (NIR-II) allows precise in vivo cell transplantation guidance. Consequently, this multifunctional NSCs therapy achieves high Aβ degradation, improved cell viability, and efficient nerve regeneration, offering an effective strategy for AD treatment. Zhang et al. introduced a cascade-targeted biomimetic nanozyme (KT-nChap@Cur) designed for the synergistic treatment of AD by addressing various pathogenic factors in the brain microenvironment (Fig. 7B) [155]. After systemic administration, KT-nChap@Cur effectively targets and crosses the BBB through interactions between the brain-targeting peptide and transferrin receptor, specifically accumulating in Aβ plaque-rich areas. It continuously releases curcumin (Cur) to depolymerize large Aβ aggregates into smaller fragments, enhancing Aβ clearance. KT-nChap@Cur exhibits metal chelation and ROS scavenging properties, regulating metal ion homeostasis and reducing oxidative stress. This approach offers a new avenue for multi-target, synergistic treatment of AD and other neurodegenerative diseases.

Yang et al. synthesized natural polyphenol nanodots (NDs) using a hydrothermal method. These NDs not only inhibit Aβ fibrillation but also mitigate Aβ cascade damage by normalizing the oxidative microenvironment, modulating microglia polarization, and protecting against neuronal death and synaptic loss [94]. More importantly, NDs exhibit stronger anti-amyloid activity compared to small molecules and can penetrate the BBB. Their excellent biocompatibility presents a promising new approach for developing AD treatment methods. Jia et al. synthesized an octahedral palladium nanozyme using a hydrothermal method and then modified the Pd NPs with enzyme activity with bifunctional polyethylene glycol (SH-PEG-COOH) to obtain Pd@PEG. Then, Pd@PEG and Bor were covalently coupled to obtain Pd@PEG@Bor [91]. This nanozyme maintains the oxidative balance in the body by mimicking the activities of POD and SOD, effectively removing excess O2 ·−and ·OH from the cells, and increasing the permeability of epithelial cells. Pd@PEG@Bor can cross the BBB and target neurons, then eliminate excessive ROS in cells, maintain mitochondrial membrane potential and Ca2+ level, inhibit the production and polymerization of Aβ, reduce neuroinflammation, protect neurons, and further improve the cognitive impairment of AD mice (Fig. 7C). Ma et al. designed a biomimetic nanozyme (CuxO@EM-K) featuring an Aβ-targeting peptide, KLVFF, which is a pentapeptide derived from Aβ. The CuxO core of this nanozyme exhibits robust CAT, SOD, and GPx activities, effectively reducing Aβ-induced oxidative damage [92]. The erythrocyte membrane coating on CuxO@EM-K enhances its biocompatibility and persistence in blood, prevents protein corona formation, and maintains its Aβ-targeting ability in biofluids (Fig. 7D). This study offers new strategies for treating neuroinflammatory and neurodegenerative diseases, including AD.

Copyright 2021, WILEY-VCH GMBH). (B) Preparation of cascade targeted multifunctional nanozyme protein and schematic diagram of AD treatment mechanism (Reproduced with permission [155]. Copyright 2024, ELSEVIER). (C) The mechanism diagram of Pd@PEG@Bor synthesis and treatment of AD (Reproduced with permission [91]. Copyright 2021, AMERICAN CHEMICAL SOCIETY). (D) Preparation of CuxO@EM-K nanozyme and schematic diagram for the treatment of AD (Reproduced with permission [92]. Copyright 2020, AMERICAN CHEMICAL SOCIETY).

Parkinson's disease (PD) is a prevalent neurodegenerative disorder characterized by bradykinesia, tremor, muscle stiffness, and progressive loss of dopaminergic neurons [156]. Although current drug and surgical treatments can alleviate motor symptoms in PD patients to some extent, an effective cure remains elusive [157]. The pathogenesis of PD is complex, involving the abnormal accumulation of active α-synuclein (α-syn), mitochondrial dysfunction, increased oxidative stress, and neuroinflammation caused by microglia [158]. These factors interact and form a vicious circle, which exacerbates the disease. Notably, reactive oxygen species (ROS) play a crucial triggering role in this process. Research indicates that an increase in α-syn levels within cells can trigger and accelerate this vicious cycle [159]. The accumulation of α-syn not only promotes the formation of pathological protein aggregates but also increases ROS production. This, in turn, further aggravates oxidative stress and accelerates neuronal damage in PD lesions [160,161].

In response to the problem of PD, nanozymes with antioxidant properties offer promising new treatment strategies. These nanozymes address oxidative stress by regulating ROS levels, potentially becoming a new approach for treating PD. For instance, Liu et al. developed a PtCu nanozyme with antioxidant properties and tested it on α-syn prefibrillar (PFF) cells and animal models [95]. In vitro experiments demonstrated that PtCu nanozymes effectively scavenged ROS in PFF-induced primary neuronal cultures. Additionally, this nanozyme achieved promising results in inhibiting α-syn diffusion, reducing neurotoxicity, and blocking pathological transmission between neurons, thus providing new treatment possibilities for PD. Building on the PtCu nanozyme, Xu et al. introduced selenium (Se) and created a new three-element nanozyme, the PtCuSe nanozyme. This nanozyme not only exhibits CAT and SOD-like activities but also shows efficient cellular uptake in neurons [96]. The PtCuSe nanozymes reduce H2O2 damage to cell tissues by catalyzing the conversion of excess H2O2 into O2. By lowering intracellular ROS levels, these nanozymes significantly improved cell activity and effectively alleviated behavioral and pathological symptoms in PD mice. Li et al. prepared liposome nanozymes (MOF@Man liposomes) using iron porphyrin (Fe–P), benzoic acid, triphenylphosphine, and a Zr6 cluster as ligands, with mannitol (Man) modification to enhance targeting. These nanozymes possess both CAT-like and SOD-like enzyme activities [97]. In a PD mouse model, the MOF@Man liposome nanozyme's shell detaches upon fusion with the blood-brain barrier, releasing Zr-FePMOF into the brain parenchyma. This release allows the nanozyme to exert antioxidant and anti-inflammatory functions by simulating enzyme-like activities, effectively inhibiting inflammation and alleviating PD symptoms (Fig. 8 A). Jiang et al. developed two novel nanozymes, Ptzyme@L-ZIF and Ptzyme@D-ZIF, by integrating nanozymes into metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [98]. These nanozymes exhibit enhanced cascade CAT and SOD mimic activities, as well as excellent brain accumulation and plasma stability, making them effective anti-neuroinflammatory agents for PD treatment. They not only cross the blood-brain barrier but also show significant therapeutic effects in eliminating ROS and reducing brain inflammation. Collectively, these studies demonstrate that nanozymes can regulate oxidative stress and neuroinflammation by mimicking intracellular antioxidant enzyme activities, offering new avenues for the clinical treatment of PD (Fig. 8C).

Li et al. developed a neuroinflammation-regulated nanoreactor, Que/Ptzyme@Man-PLGA (QPMP), by encapsulating dihydroquercetin (Que) and platinum nanozyme in mannitol-modified polylactic acid-glycolic acid copolymer (PLGA) nanoparticles [99]. By modifying the surface of QPMP with mannitol, the nanoreactor was able to target the BBB and accumulate in neurons and microglia. This dual-synergistic nanoreactor effectively combines anti-inflammatory small molecule compounds with nanozymes. In PD cells and animal models, the nanoreactor successfully crosses the BBB, reduces neuronal oxidative damage, and promotes the polarization of microglia to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype, thus achieving a synergistic treatment for PD. Gao et al. constructed an integrated nanoreactor, Que@CeO2-BSA-GSH (Q@CeBG), by encapsulating CeO2 nanozyme and Que in glutathione-modified bovine serum albumin (GSH-modified BSA) [100]. Using focused ultrasound (FUS) technology, they temporarily opened the BBB, which enhanced the accumulation of Q@CeBG in the brain. Assisted by FUS, the Q@CeBG nanoreactor could penetrate the BBB to act on neurons and microglia, reducing oxidative stress in neurons and promoting the transformation of microglia from the pro-inflammatory M1 to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. The Q@CeBG nanoreactor demonstrates excellent biocompatibility and offers significant neuroprotective and immunomodulatory effects, positioning it as a promising platform for combating PD.

Studies have shown that PD may lead to mitochondrial dysfunction and excessive release of ROS, which in turn activates the nucleotide-binding oligomerization domain (NLRP3) inflammasome in microglia and triggers the aggregation and propagation of α-syn [162]. Therefore, utilizing antioxidant nanozymes to eliminate excess ROS in mitochondria could be a promising strategy to block NLRP3-mediated α-syn aggregation and mitigate PD [163].The Prussian blue nanozyme (PBzyme), synthesized by Ma et al. demonstrated potential in reducing neurodegeneration in PD mice and cell models of motor dysfunction induced by 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) [101]. Leveraging its exceptional ROS scavenging properties and catalytic activity, PBzyme reduced dopaminergic degeneration and inhibited neuroinflammation through intraventricular injection in the MPTP-induced PD mouse model. This intervention alleviated movement disorders (Fig. 8B) and PD symptoms by mitigating mitochondrial membrane potential damage. Free antioxidant enzymes face challenges such as easy inactivation, short half-life, and low transmembrane transport capacity, which often limit their clinical effectiveness [164]. To address these issues, Fan et al. incorporated nanozymes into thermosensitive hydrogels (PLGA-PEG-PLGA) to create an antioxidant nanozyme system (Cu-ZIF@Hydrogel) [102]. Cu-ZIF@Hydrogel could cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) at both cellular and animal levels, effectively accumulating in nerve cells and significantly alleviating behavioral and pathological symptoms of PD by reducing neuroinflammation at the lesion site (Fig. 8D). Wang et al. developed a polydopamine-based composite nanozyme (PDASeCys) with GPx, CAT, and SOD-like activities [103]. During oxidative stress, CAT removes excessive H2O2, while GPx regulates H2O2 concentration to facilitate cell signaling. PDASeCys effectively increases intracellular GPx levels, scavenges ROS, reduces oxidative stress, and alleviates PD-related symptoms in mice.

The wound healing process is often compromised by adverse factors such as tissue hypoxia, inflammatory response disorders, and reduced collagen production. These factors interact to cause the accumulation of ROS and exacerbate oxidative stress [165]. In the wound healing environment, excessive oxidative stress induced by inflammation or hypoxia is a critical barrier to effective healing. Elevated ROS levels not only worsen inflammation but also cause DNA damage and cellular aging, severely impeding the natural healing process. Antioxidant enzymes naturally present in the human body play a crucial role in maintaining physiological balance by scavenging ROS and protecting against oxidative damage. Therefore, developing nanozymes that can mimic the activity of these natural antioxidant enzymes may significantly enhance ROS scavenging in wounds, reduce inflammatory responses, and promote wound healing.

Hu et al. successfully developed an ultrafine gold nanozyme with POD-like activity. This nanozyme effectively decomposes H2O2 into highly reactive ·OH and exhibits significant antibacterial effects against Staphylococcus aureus, which is crucial for promoting wound healing [166]. However, this nanozyme had limitations in catalytic efficiency, ROS stability, and range of action, which constrain its overall effectiveness in sterilization. To address these limitations and enhance the performance of nanozymes, Zhang et al. designed and constructed a near-infrared light-responsive antibacterial nanozyme, CuS@Pt–Au/Apt [104]. This nanozyme improves antibacterial efficacy and promotes wound healing through a synergistic approach combining targeting, chemical kinetic therapy (CDT), and photothermal therapy (PTT). The CuS@Pt–Au/Apt nanozymes specifically target bacterial surfaces, utilize the thermal effect generated by near-infrared light, and leverage the high glucose concentration in diabetic infected wounds to produce ·OH via a cascade nanozyme reaction. Additionally, the strong oxidation stress caused by Cu2+ released from copper sulfide disrupts the bacterial antioxidant defense system. This process effectively removes ROS, mitigates the inflammatory response, and inflicts severe and irreversible damage on bacteria, thereby significantly enhancing the effectiveness of antimicrobial therapy (Fig. 9 D). Gao et al. developed a phot-crosslinked hyaluronic acid (HAMA)-based material that integrates an asymmetric MnO2–Au-mSiO2 Janus nanobiocatalyst system with an injectable hydrogel (HAMA-mSAM@aFGF) coated with fibroblast growth factor (aFGF) [105]. This innovative nanobiocatalyst effectively mimics the enzyme activity of CAT and SOD, neutralize excessive ROS and reduce inflammation, and can also stimulate cell proliferation and angiogenesis. These characteristics promote the transition of the wound from the inflammatory phase to the proliferative phase. The development and application of this new, multifunctional and injectable nano-enzyme hydrogel system provides a novel perspective for accurately regulating the healing process of diabetic wounds.

To address the complications of chronic wounds, particularly in diabetic wound healing, Nandin Mandakhbayar et al. developed a novel bioactive nanozyme based on cobalt-doped nanoglass (CoNZ) [89]. This nanozyme not only exhibits efficient enzyme-like catalytic activity but also releases therapeutic ions, providing dual functions of antioxidation and angiogenesis. CoNZ reacts with H2O2 through in situ-generated cobalt oxide (Co3O4) crystallites, effectively removing ROS, reducing pro-inflammatory markers, and promoting wound healing by enhancing cell proliferation and activating angiogenic factors. In a mouse model of streptococcal toxin-induced diabetes, CoNZ nanozymes accelerated wound healing, promoted new angiogenesis, matrix deposition, and epithelial cell arrangement, while reducing inflammation. The effect of CoNZ on diabetic wound closure is comparable to that of the clinically available drug desferrimycin, suggesting that CoNZ has the potential to become a novel drug-free treatment [167]. Cheng et al. proposed an antioxidant nanozyme (TA-Cu) that combines tannic acid (TA) with two-dimensional copper (Cu NS) through phenolic ligand-metal charge transfer (LMCT) to eliminate ROS and promote wound healing in chronic diabetes [107]. The coordination of polyphenol ligands on copper phosphate (Cu3(PO4)2) nanosheets facilitates strong charge transfer at the interface and regulates the valence state of Cu. TA coordination harnesses the cytotoxic effects of Cu nanozymes while protecting cells from oxidative damage by decomposing ROS. This nanozyme enhances diabetic wound healing by promoting re-epithelialization, collagen deposition, angiogenesis, and immune regulation. Zhou et al. developed a nanohybrid double network hydrogel based on a platinum nanozyme complex (PFOB@PLGA@Pt) to address the chronic microenvironmental characteristics of diabetic wounds [108]. In the initial stage of wound healing, PFOB@PLGA@Pt improved the hypoxic environment and activated a synergistic antibacterial effect by providing oxygen and simulating the catalytic activity of various enzymes (e.g., GOx, CAT, and OXD). As bacterial infection clearance transitioned into the second stage, the CAT-like and SOD-like activities of Pt nanozymes reshaped the redox microenvironment, cleared excessive ROS, and facilitated the transition of the wound from the inflammatory phase to the proliferative phase. This microenvironment-adaptive hydrogel therapy addresses all stages of wound healing and significantly enhances the repair of diabetic infected wounds (Fig. 9A).

Nanomaterials, such as metal oxides and carbon-based nanoparticles, offer new opportunities for enhancing wound healing due to their unique properties and versatile enzyme-like activities. Du et al. developed a novel pH-responsive nanozyme, Fe3O4-Gox nanoparticles coated with a GOx shell, enabling temporal and spatial regulation of the pathological wound microenvironment [109]. The Fe3O4-GOx nanozymes exhibit GOx-like, CAT, and POD-like activities, promoting wound healing through pH-responsive GOx/POD and GOx/CAT cascade reactions under acidic and neutral conditions, respectively. Under acidic conditions, the GOx/POD cascade reaction generates continuous ·OH that specifically target and remove acidic biofilms (pH ∼5.5), thus reducing the inflammatory period and initiating normal wound healing (Fig. 9C). Conversely, the continuous O2 produced by the GOx/CAT cascade reaction under neutral conditions targets the neutral wound tissue, accelerating the proliferation and remodeling stages of wound healing by addressing issues of hyperglycemia, hypoxia, and excessive oxidative stress. Yang et al. developed an arginine-loaded nanozyme (FTA) based on metal-polyphenol materials for wound healing [168]. This nanozyme mimics CAT activity to remove excess ROS at the wound site while producing oxygen and supplying NO precursors through arginine. NO, metabolized from arginine, promotes angiogenesis, facilitates the formation of early transport channels, and further enhances oxygen supply. Additionally, FTA reduces inflammation by upregulating cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) and downregulating nuclear transcription factor (NF-κB), shifting macrophage phenotype from pro-inflammatory M1 to anti-inflammatory M2, thereby improving wound healing. Wang et al. constructed a nanozyme with CAT-like activity, EGAP@HG, which integrates hydrogel bio adhesives to improve the microenvironment of diabetic ulcers [110]. The gallic acid released from the outer layer of the EGAP@HG nanozyme exhibits anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, while Ag⁺ released from its internal template provide a bactericidal effect (Fig. 9B). Additionally, in a hypoxic environment, the EGAP nanozyme decomposes H₂O₂ to produce O₂, alleviating local hypoxia. The continuous release of epidermal growth factor (EGF) promotes epithelial cell proliferation. These properties make EGAP@HG nanozyme effective in enhancing the local microenvironment of diabetic wounds and offer a promising new therapeutic strategy for diabetic wound healing, with potential for future clinical applications.

Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a common chronic inflammatory disease characterized by persistent synovitis, which leads to joint pain, stiffness, and loss of function [169]. A key feature of RA is the activation of synovial cells (FLS) and the infiltration of monocytes that differentiate into macrophages, contributing to the disease's persistence [170]. Despite ongoing research, current therapeutic approaches for RA have not achieved optimal results, highlighting the need to explore new treatment strategies to halt disease progression. In the pathological process of RA, the accumulation of ROS serves as a significant marker. ROS causes substantial disruption of cellular metabolism and mitochondrial function within the harsh RA microenvironment, which exacerbates ROS and inflammatory cytokine levels and negatively impacts the effectiveness of RA treatment [171].

Neutrophils accumulate in the early inflammatory stages of RA, respond to inflammatory signals, and release pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-1β (IL-1β), and interleukin-6 (IL-6). This leads to increased ROS, resulting in cartilage destruction and bone erosion [172]. To address these challenges, Zhang et al. developed a 5 nm ultra-small Prussian blue (uPB-Exo) functionalized neutrophil-derived exosome nanozyme (Fig. 10 A). This nanozyme retains the targeting capabilities of neutrophils and exhibits excellent anti-inflammatory properties [111]. In a late-stage RA mouse model, uPB-Exo selectively accumulates in synovial cells, neutralizes pro-inflammatory factors, and reduces the inflammatory environment deliver the anti-inflammatory drug rhein (RH) to pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages in the inflammatory synovial tissue. Zhou et al. created the (HA@RH-CeOX) nanoreactor platform by the complexation between CeO2 nanozymes and hyaluronic acid (HA) biopolymers [112]. It accurately delivers nanozymes and rheumatoid arthritis drug Rhein (RH) to pro-inflammatory macrophages in inflammatory synovial tissue, and RH inhibits TLR4 signaling in these macrophages. At the same time, the Ce3+/Ce4+ redox couple showed sod-like activity in M1 macrophages, rapidly decomposing ROS, alleviating oxidative stress, reducing local inflammation, and promoting cartilage repair. It is worth noting that intra-articular injection of HA@RH-CeOX in arthritis rats can significantly reduce the levels of inflammatory factors TNF-α and IL-6, effectively regenerate cartilage and restore joint function. The nanozyme-mediated redox homeostasis strategy can effectively reprogram M1 macrophages in the RA microenvironment into M2 phenotype to reduce its pro-inflammatory potential to accelerate joint repair, which provides a method for the clinical treatment of arthritis. However, the SOD-like activity of Ce-based nanozymes can be compromised in high H2O2 oxidation environments. Gong et al. designed Ce and Se co-doped carbon dots (CS-CDs) nanozymes as a transfer vector for Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 to inhibit and image miRNA-155 in M1 macrophages [113]. The Ce active center simulates SOD activity by catalyzing O2 ·- to H2O2, while the adjacent Se catalyzes the conversion of H2O2 by simulating GPx, achieving enhanced catalysis and superior ROS scavenging ability (Fig. 10C). Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 forms a double-stranded hybrid with the complementary base of miRNA-155, effectively inhibiting the biological regulatory function of miRNA-155. This process promotes the polarization of M2 macrophages, breaks the vicious cycle between inflammation and ROS, and enables synergistic treatment of RA.

It has been reported that blocking the feedback loop of TNF-α and IL-1β can restore the normal metabolic balance of O2 ·- and H2O2. Based on this discovery, Jia et al. combined MnO2 with microbubbles derived from macrophages (MMV) and loaded dexamethasone sodium phosphate (DSP) on its surface to synthesize nanozymes with both SOD-like and CAT-like activities, termed MMV-MnO2@DSP [114]. MMV-MnO2@DSP demonstrated an excellent ability to target inflammatory synovitis in a RA rat model. It accumulated in activated macrophages, restored the metabolic balance of O2 ·- and H2O2, and promoted repolarization between macrophages, fibroblast-like synoviocytes, and chondrocytes. Additionally, MMV-MnO2@DSP inhibited the feedback loop of TNF-α and IL-1β, effectively reprogrammed the RA inflammatory joint microenvironment, and exhibited significant anti-RA effects both in vitro and in vivo (Fig. 10D). Zhao et al. drew inspiration from biological metabolism to develop a hydrogel doped with MnCoO nanozymes [115]. This hydrogel, characterized by its excellent CAT mimetic enzyme activity within a three-dimensional polymer network, also exhibits injectability, self-healing properties, and biocompatibility. It serves as an injectable delivery vector for bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs), protecting the implanted cells from ROS and hypoxia-mediated death. This application alleviates RA symptoms by inhibiting local inflammatory cytokines and improving osseointegration (Fig. 10B). Hao et al. developed three biomimetic nanozymes (Biomimetic NPs) from different cell types for dual targeting of inflammatory sites and M1 macrophages [173]. These nanozymes effectively treat RA through dual-targeted drug delivery to inflammatory joints and by inducing the transformation of macrophages from M1 to M2. The NPs accumulated at the inflammatory sites of M1 macrophages with high internalization efficiency. After loading methotrexate, the NPs significantly inhibited the viability of activated macrophages and reduced the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines. In an adjuvant-induced arthritis mouse model, NPs effectively decreased the number of inflammatory macrophages, the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and the destruction of cartilage and bone tissue.

Liu et al. developed a Janus nanoplatform, named Janus-cps, which features CeO2–Pt nanozyme subunits on one side and mesoporous organosilicon (PMO) subunits on the other [116]. This platform is designed to load aconitine lactone (MCL) drugs for early RA diagnosis and synergistic nanozymes treatment. The Janus nanostructure increases the exposure of active sites and enhances the ability of CeO2–Pt nanozyme subunits to scavenge ROS. Furthermore, Janus-CPS, loaded with indocyanine green (ICG), employs NIR-II fluorescence imaging to monitor the onset and progression of RA in mouse models at an early stage. This Janus nanoplatform is anticipated to offer a novel strategy for multifunctional therapy through functional integration. Hou et al. reported a protein-crosslinked microsphere nanozyme (NTMs) with both CAT and POD activities. This nanozyme can eliminate endogenous overexpressed ROS and produce dissolved oxygen synergistically [117]. NTMs facilitate the polarization of inflammatory macrophages from M1 to M2 and regulate joint redox homeostasis, thereby alleviating RA symptoms. NTMs exhibit good biosafety, are engulfed by macrophages, and subsequently localize in the cytoplasm. In vivo studies with capsaicin-loaded NTMs demonstrated improved cartilage structure and organized nuclear structure, resembling healthy natural cartilage. These findings suggest that NTMs, by mimicking oxidoreductase strategies, can regulate the joint microenvironment, while capsaicin provides synergistic anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, indicating high potential for RA treatment. Lu et al. developed a photocatalytic biomimetic nanozyme (CuS@ZnS) that modulates innate immunity by producing hydrogen and restores the immune system by reducing lactic acid (LA), addressing various RA factors [174]. The heterostructure of plasma copper and zinc monosulfide was utilized to explore abnormal plasma-induced electron transfer during the NIR-induced hydrogen evolution reaction. In this study, overexpressed LA was used as a sacrificial agent for near-infrared photocatalytic therapy. The generated hydrogen scavenges intracellular reactive oxygen species, regulates macrophage polarization from M1 to M2, and inhibits cytokine storms, thus reducing inflammation in collagen-induced mouse models. This “two-in-one” photocatalytic biomimetic nanozyme strategy, combining LA consumption with targeted hydrogen delivery, presents a potential approach for treating progressive arthritis.

Osteoarthritis (OA) is a degenerative disease affecting the entire joint. Its main characteristics include articular cartilage degeneration, inflammatory response, and resulting chronic pain and stiffness [175,176]. Over time, changes occur in the periosteum, joint capsule, and muscle ligaments surrounding the joint, leading to increased biological stress on the joint surface. This stress accelerates articular cartilage degradation, creating a vicious cycle that exacerbates the disease process [177]. In clinical practice, OA treatment typically involves both non-drug and drug therapies. While these treatments can alleviate pain and symptoms, they do not halt disease progression and can have side effects, including damage to the digestive and cardiovascular systems [178]. Currently, effective treatments for OA are limited. A notable aspect of osteoarthritis is the persistent damage to articular cartilage, which involves chondrocyte death and extracellular matrix degradation. This damage is primarily due to an imbalance in the articular cartilage microenvironment and excessive ROS accumulation in the joint [179]. Excessive ROS leads to oxidative stress and disrupts the cellular antioxidant system [180]. Additionally, hypoxia and high ROS levels result in increased hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF-1α) levels, which further promotes synovial inflammation, regulates macrophage polarization, and induces the transformation of M1 macrophages. Therefore, employing nanozymes with antioxidant properties to balance macrophage subtypes and reduce hypoxia and ROS levels may offer an effective strategy for OA treatment [181].

Excessive production of ROS and injury-related cytokines activates multiple signaling pathways, accelerating chondrocyte decomposition, metabolism, and apoptosis. Hou et al. synthesized hollow Prussian blue nanozymes (HPBzymes) using a hydrothermal method, demonstrating significant therapeutic effects in OA rat models [118]. HPBzymes effectively remodeled the microenvironment by reducing Rac1 expression and ROS levels, and by preventing the release and nuclear transfer of nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB). In mice, HPBzymes protected chondrocytes, reduced inflammation, and prevented the degradation of the extracellular matrix (ECM) of chondrocytes, proving effective in treating OA (Fig. 11 A). Chen et al. synthesized hollow MnO2 nanozymes (H–MnO2-PEG) via the Stöber modification method, which not only targeted and treated OA effectively but also exhibited excellent biological stability and biocompatibility [119]. In vivo studies, including knee joint tissue staining and total knee joint micro-CT scans, confirmed that H–MnO2-PEG nanoparticles effectively removed ROS, repaired articular cartilage structure, and significantly alleviated OA inflammation, providing a new approach for OA treatment. Wang et al. designed a manganese tetroxide nanozyme hydrogel (Mn3O4@CS) with SOD-like and CAT-like activities to mitigate oxidative stress damage [120]. In vitro experiments showed that Mn3O4 nanozymes could rebalance the metabolic state of chondrocytes and control the inflammatory response induced by H2O2. In vivo, Mn3O4@CS hydrogel, used as a carrier for Mn3O4 nanozymes, showed therapeutic effects on cartilage metabolism and inflammation in mouse osteoarthritis models. This suggests that Mn3O4 nanozyme-based strategies may offer a novel treatment approach for OA patients. Li et al. combined cobalt sulfonated phthalocyanine (CoPcS) and titanium carbide (Ti3C2Tx) nanosheets through non-covalent π-π coupling to load ultrafine Pd nanoclusters, and developed Pd/CoPcS-Ti3C2Tx nanozymes [121]. The electron work function of Pd/CoPcS-Ti3C2Tx nanozyme is lower than that of Ti3C2Tx, and the photothermal conversion efficiency (49.1 %) and enzyme catalytic activity are improved. In vitro experiments showed that the Pd/CoPcS-Ti3C2Tx antioxidant system mimicking SOD/CAT could effectively eliminate ROS and RNS, inhibit inflammatory factors, prevent energy deficiency, regulate mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake, and restore mitochondrial function, thus effectively treating OA.

Recent studies indicate that MOF nanozymes have significant potential in antioxidant therapy and oxygen supply due to their strong CAT-like activity. Yu et al. developed a copper-based metal-organic framework (Cu MOF) nanozyme for OA treatment. The Cu MOF nanozyme demonstrated both SOD-like and CAT-like activities and effectively scavenged ·OH.

[122]. Compared to other copper-based antioxidants such as CuO and Cu monoatomic nanozymes, Cu MOF is more effective in scavenging various ROS (Fig. 11B). Moreover, Cu MOF's efficient oxygen production capacity alleviates the hypoxic microenvironment of synovial macrophages in OA patients. This dual effect promotes the transformation of macrophages from pro-inflammatory M1 to anti-inflammatory M2 types and reduces the secretion of pro-inflammatory factors. Xu et al. developed a NIR-sensitive tea polyphenol-modified zirconium porphyrin MOF (TP-Au@PCN) nanozyme as a multifunctional nanoplatform for OA treatment [123]. Under near-infrared irradiation, TP-Au@PCN alleviated cellular oxidative stress by promoting extracellular matrix expression in chondrocytes and repairing mitochondrial damage. Molecular biology results indicated that TP-Au@PCN reduced chondrocyte apoptosis, increased chondrocyte generation, and improved the articular cavity microenvironment to treat OA. Guo et al. prepared KGN@HMZC@HA nanozyme using a simple two-step method. The oxygen vacancies formed during Zn doping endowed KGN@HMZC@HA with SOD and CAT catalytic activities [124]. This nanozyme controls KGN release based on the specific OA environment, achieving chemically programmed OA microenvironment remodeling. It effectively removes ROS, produces O2, promotes macrophage transformation from M1 to M2, and inhibits inflammation (Fig. 11C).Zhang et al. used methoxy polyethylene glycol-thiol grafting (mPEG-TK) modified zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) nanozyme to treat OA [125]. At high ROS levels, the mPEG-TK coating on ZIF-8 nanozymes was depolymerized, allowing ROS to activate Cell ROX fluorescence for OA diagnosis and monitoring. Simultaneously, resveratrol (RES) was released from the nanoparticles, stimulating macrophage polarization from M1 to M2 (Fig. 11D). These nanoparticles inhibited inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS), reduced NO production, and mitigated the inflammatory response caused by M1 macrophages, thereby reducing cartilage degradation and integrating diagnosis and treatment of OA inflammation.

Repairing mitochondrial function is an effective strategy for treating OA. This approach involves targeting mitochondrial homeostasis, reducing local oxidative stress, overcoming drug resistance, and enhancing therapeutic effects [182]. Xiang et al. prepared Pt single atom (Pt SA/C3N4) nanozyme loaded with g-C3N4 through a ligand-mediated strategy. The Pt–N6 active center in Pt SA/C3N4 forms an electron capture site for electron holes. Among them, g-C3N4 regulates the d-band center of Pt, and the N-rich site transfers electrons to Pt, thereby enhancing the adsorption of free radicals, thus having good SOD/CAT-like activity, which can scavenge ROS/RNS, regulate the production of mitochondrial ATP, and delay the progress of OA [126]. Li et al. developed a multifunctional Au@CeO2 yolk-shell nanozyme (YSN) as a novel therapeutic nanomaterial and drug delivery system [127]. Under photothermal therapy (PTT), Au@CeO2 nanozyme neutralizes excessive ROS and maintains mitochondrial stability in OA chondrocytes. The bioactive peptide CK2.1 loaded onto YSNs (Au@CeO2-CK2.1) is released in an orderly manner under photothermal conditions, effectively promoting chondrocyte anabolic activity (Fig. 12 A). Additionally, mitochondrial dynamics are regulated by inhibiting the phosphorylation of extracellular regulated protein kinases (ERK1/2) and dynein-1-like protein (DRP1). This study demonstrates an effective treatment strategy that rescues the cartilage drug response in OA chondrocytes, achieving anti-inflammatory effects and cartilage regeneration. Yang et al. synthesized a free radical adsorption nanozyme (Pd Zn/CoSA-NC) with high electron transfer and fully exposed double active sites, which reduces reaction energy and accelerates catalytic reactions with SOD-like, CAT-like, and GPx-like activities, outperforming CoSA-NC and Pd Zn/NC alone [183]. Pd Zn/CoSA-NC nanozymes also exhibited good biocompatibility, reducing oxidative stress in OA synovial macrophages, alleviating hypoxia, restoring mitochondrial function, regulating energy metabolism, increasing antioxidant factors, and decreasing inflammatory factors (Fig. 12C). Mechanistically, Pd Zn/CoSA-NC nanozymes downregulate M1 phenotype markers such as IL-1β by regulating purine metabolism. This nanozyme offers a novel approach for treating oxidative stress-related inflammatory diseases. Wei et al. developed a mixed Pt–Se nanozyme, which exhibits stronger ROS scavenging ability compared to Pt and Se nanozymes alone, providing a synergistic effect for OA treatment (Fig. 12B) [128]. Pt–Se nanozymes possess SOD and CAT activities, promoting the polarization of M1 macrophages to the M2 phenotype, inhibiting the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, and repairing mitochondrial function in arthritic chondrocytes. In vivo results also demonstrated that Pt–Se nanozymes effectively inhibited the early progression of OA. This approach highlights the potential of leveraging the inherent biological activity of nanozymes to modify the microenvironment, offering a promising strategy for OA and other ROS-related chronic diseases. Qin et al. synthesized an ultrasmall Prussian blue nanozyme (USPBNPs) by regulating the configuration of the polyvinylpyrrolidone chain [184]. USPBNPs demonstrated excellent ROS scavenging ability and CAT-like activity, capable of decomposing H2O2 into O2. The anti-inflammatory mechanism can be attributed to the repolarization of macrophages from the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype by reducing ROS levels and improving O2. Additionally, USPBNPs exhibited exciting therapeutic effects in vivo. This study not only developed a new therapeutic agent for osteoarthritis but also provided an estimable insight into the application of nanozymes.

Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), which includes ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD), is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by symptoms such as diarrhea, abdominal pain, hematochezia, and various extraintestinal manifestations like arthritis, psoriasis, and spondylitis. These symptoms significantly impact patients' quality of life [185]. A key feature of IBD is the excessive accumulation of ROS in the body.
