Elevated ROS levels can damage the innate antioxidant system, oxidize biomolecules (such as proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids), and cause intestinal epithelial injury [186]. These processes exacerbate inflammation by increasing the infiltration of inflammatory cells, enhancing ROS and inflammatory cytokine production, and establishing a vicious cycle between ROS and inflammation, which accelerates disease progression. Although exogenous natural antioxidant enzymes have been used to neutralize ROS or inhibit inflammation in IBD treatment, their clinical application is limited due to issues with stability, high cost, and potential immunogenicity [187,188].

Nanomaterials, particularly nanozymes with enzyme-like catalytic activity, have garnered significant attention as potential candidates for regulating redox homeostasis in IBD. Li et al. developed Au/CeO2@HA nanozymes by embedding cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2) into gold nanoparticles and coating them with negatively charged hyaluronic acid (HA). This nanozyme treats IBD by enhancing the activity of SOD and CAT enzymes to remove excessive ROS [129]. The Au/CeO2 nanozyme features a core-shell and porous structure, which provides more catalytic sites, a higher content of Ce(III), and increased oxygen vacancies, thereby accelerating the reduction of Ce(IV) to Ce(III) (Fig. 13 A). After oral administration, Au/CeO₂@HA accumulates in inflammatory colon tissue, reducing the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and effectively alleviating colon injury in mice with acute colitis. Excessive ROS accumulation leads to oxidative damage to DNA, proteins, and lipids, contributing to inflammatory IBD. Yang et al. developed a thermosensitive hydrogel-based nanozyme (MLPPP) for targeted therapy of IBD (Fig. 13C) [130]. This system incorporates Mn₃O₄ into a thermosensitive hydrogel (PDLLA-PEG-PDLLA), endowing MLPPP with various enzyme activities, including CAT, GPx, and SOD activities. Following colorectal administration, MLPPP nanozymes target the inflammatory colon, forming a physical protective barrier and continuously releasing Mn3O4 to scavenge ROS. MLPPP also maintains the intestinal barrier by directly scavenging ROS, reconstructing the cellular antioxidant system, exerting anti-inflammatory effects, up-regulating tight junction (TJ) proteins, and promoting epithelial cell apoptosis.

Additionally, an imbalance in intestinal flora plays a crucial role in IBD, with an increase in Enterobacteriaceae facultative anaerobes (such as E. coli) and a decrease in beneficial bacteria observed in patients’ intestines. Recent studies have highlighted that regulating ROS levels and reprogramming microbial respiration and energy metabolism are effective strategies for treating IBD [189]. Qin et al. developed tungsten oxide nanozymes (WO3) that regulate intestinal flora by inhibiting molybdenum cofactors. Compared to free tungsten (sodium tungstate), WO3 nanozymes significantly reduce intestinal inflammation, bacterial translocation, and restore the colonic epithelial barrier. They inhibit the growth of Enterobacteriaceae bacteria through microbial metabolic reprogramming, thereby reshaping the homeostasis of intestinal microbiota in the inflammatory colon and improving colitis [190]. Jiang et al. synthesized Pluronic F-127 modified tungsten diselenide (WSe2@F127) nanozymes using liquid phase exfoliation technology. These nanozymes can scavenge RONS through the valence transition of Se2⁻/Se⁴⁺ and W⁴⁺/W⁶⁺ [131]. Oral administration of WSe2@F127 improves UC induced by dextran sodium sulfate (DSS). In the inflammatory area, high concentrations of RONS promote the release of tungsten ions, restore epithelial barrier function, and reduce intestinal inflammation, thereby reshaping the intestinal microbial community. In CD models, WSe2@F127 reduces ROS levels in T cells, inhibits T cell differentiation, and significantly decreases interferon production (Fig. 13B). This reduces oxidative stress damage, regulates intestinal microbial communities, and reshapes immune barriers, effectively alleviating IBD. Huang et al. proposed a metal-free melanin nanozyme (Me NPs) that offers good gastrointestinal stability and biocompatibility as an effective strategy for treating IBD [132]. Me nanozymes exhibit long-lasting ability to target IBD lesions. Following oral administration, negatively charged Me nanozymes target the inflammatory site in the colon through electrostatic interactions, eliminate oxidative stress by scavenging ROS, protect mitochondria, and reduce endoplasmic reticulum stress by decreasing PERK (RNA-like endoplasmic reticulum kinase), eIF-2α (eukaryotic initiation factor), and ATF4 (transcription factor). This restoration of the intestinal barrier contributes to the normalization of intestinal flora. Ma et al. synthesized carbon dots (C-dots) nanozymes using a solvothermal method with glutathione and biotin for IBD treatment. These C-dots exhibit excellent SOD-like activity, hydroxyl radical scavenging ability, and fluorescence characteristics [133]. C-dots can eliminate excessive ROS, protect cells from oxidative stress, and reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β) (Fig. 13D). In IBD mouse models, C-dots administration shortens colon length, reduces epithelial damage and inflammatory cell infiltration, and lowers pro-inflammatory factor levels, effectively alleviating colon inflammation. The development of biomaterials like C-dots represents a promising new treatment for IBD, offering potential for long-term inflammation relief.

Yan et al. synthesized a nanomedicine (PM@Pic/PtCD@NP) composed of platinum-doped carbon dot (PtCD) nanozymes with SOD and CAT cascade enzyme activity, co-loaded with the spleen tyrosine kinase (SYK) inhibitor picetannol, and encapsulated in PLGA, then coated with a platelet membrane [134]. This formulation allows the nanozymes to co-localize with mitochondria, effectively removing ROS and reducing the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines. The biomimetic platelet membrane modification enables PM@Pic/PtCD@NP to transport neutrophils to the inflammatory site and release picetannol to inhibit neutrophil aggregation (Fig. 14 A). This treatment strategy may offer an innovative approach for achieving long-term remission of UC. Li et al. reported a multifunctional ultra-small tungsten-gallic acid (W-GA) nanozyme that addresses oxidative stress and its impact on intestinal flora [135]. By inhibiting the abnormal expansion of Enterobacteriaceae during colitis, W-GA nanozymes accurately regulate intestinal microflora and restore intestinal barrier function. Administered either orally or intravenously, W-GA nanozymes demonstrated high safety in vivo and effectively reduced colon injury in DSS-induced colitis mice (Fig. 14B). This nanozyme integrates microbial reprogramming with RONS clearance, offering a promising therapeutic strategy for intestinal inflammation. Zhang et al. successfully developed a hollow microstructured zinc-tannic acid (Zn-TA) nanozyme (HZn-TA) for treating IBD [136]. In an acute colitis model, HZn-TA demonstrated effective ROS (O2 ·⁻) and NO scavenging, mimicking SOD and CAT activities. This resulted in reduced colon damage, lowered levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines, decreased spleen index, and improved body weight, which collectively alleviated intestinal inflammation in mice. HZn-TA also promoted intestinal mucosa healing and upregulated the expression of ZO-1 and claudin-1 (Fig. 14C). Cui et al. developed highly biocompatible iodine-copper-zinc covalently doped carbon dots (Cu, Zn, I-CDs) nanozymes with CAT, SOD, and GPx-like catalytic activities to treat oxidative stress-related conditions, including IBD [137]. The iodine dopant mitigates the positive charge from copper and zinc dopants due to low pH, resulting in enhanced triple antioxidant activity compared to Cu, Zn-CDs alone. In vitro experiments showed that Cu, Zn, I-CDs effectively scavenged excessive ROS, protected cells from oxidative stress, and reduced the expression of pro-inflammatory factors TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6. In a DSS-induced colitis mouse model, Cu, Zn, I-CDs nanozymes alleviated colon inflammation, evidenced by reduced colon length, epithelial damage, inflammatory cell infiltration, and upregulated antioxidant gene expression. This provides a novel strategy for UC treatment.

Ischemic stroke (IS) accounts for 71 % of global stroke cases and is a neurological deficit syndrome caused by local blood circulation disorders in the brain. Current clinical treatments for IS primarily involve intravenous thrombolysis or intra-arterial thrombectomy. However, performing these procedures outside the optimal treatment window can increase the risk of BBB disruption and cerebral hemorrhage, leading to reperfusion injury. This can result in higher disability rates and mortality associated with IS [191]. Reperfusion injury poses a significant challenge in treating IS, as it often exacerbates the condition. Research indicates that ischemia-reperfusion injury can induce oxidative stress through various mechanisms, including free radical damage, severe inflammation, intracellular calcium overload, BBB destruction, and changes in glial cells. These processes collectively trigger and amplify inflammatory responses [192].

To address brain injury caused by blood reperfusion, both single and multifunctional nanozymes with the ability to scavenge ROS and mitigate oxidative stress have garnered significant attention [193]. Huang et al. developed a multifunctional HSA-Mn3O4 nanozyme by modifying Mn3O4 with human serum albumin (HSA). This modification extended the nanozyme's circulation time in vivo, enhanced its ROS scavenging ability, and reduced inflammation. The HSA-Mn3O4 nanozyme effectively improved ischemia-reperfusion injury and treated ischemic stroke (IS) by inhibiting apoptosis induced by oxygen and glucose deprivation (Fig. 15 A) [138]. Wang et al. created a selenium-containing bifunctional cross-linking agent using a ‘neutrophil’-loaded bovine serum albumin (BSA) nanoparticle system. This one-step synthesis produced BSA-co-hybridized nanoscale bienzymes (h-ANEs) with CAT and SOD activities. Once in the brain tissue, h-ANEs scavenged ROS through a cascade reaction. The released selenium promoted the expression of GPX4, inhibited neuronal ferroptosis, reduced cerebral infarction, and treated IS [139]. Feng et al. synthesized a non-invasive targeted mesoporous Prussian blue nanozyme (MPBzyme@NCM) using a neutrophil-like cell membrane (NCM). This nanozyme actively targeted inflamed brain microvascular endothelial cells, facilitating delivery to the damaged brain. MPBzyme@NCM's ROS scavenging activity alleviated ischemic injury and improved neurological function [140]. Huang et al. also developed multifunctional mesoporous nanozymes (Ce/Zeo-NMs) by doping cerium (Ce) into Linde Type A (LTA) zeolite-based nanomaterials. These nanozymes were used to address neurovascular unit (NVU) dysfunction and reduce reperfusion injury. In an ischemic stroke model, Ce/Zeo-NMs exhibited ROS scavenging activity and facilitated Zn2+ adsorption, which lowered intracellular Zn2⁺ levels, alleviated brain damage, and improved neurological function (Fig. 15B). Ce/Zeo-NMs demonstrated SOD-like and CAT-like catalytic activities by switching between Ce3+ and Ce4+ states, thereby reducing infarct size, preventing tight junction protein (TJP) degradation, and inhibiting microglia and astrocyte activation. This protection of the BBB significantly reduced ischemic injury to the NVU [141].

In addition to single nanozymes, multifunctional nanozymes offer a promising strategy for treating IS. Tian et al. developed antioxidant nanozymes (Fe2NC@Se) that simulate natural antioxidant systems to combat IS [142]. In vitro studies demonstrated that Fe2NC@Se mitigated oxidative stress-induced damage and inhibited apoptosis by scavenging excessive ROS. In a middle cerebral artery occlusion (MCAO) model of IS in rats, this nanozyme significantly improved the morphological deterioration caused by MCAO by inhibiting the ASK1/JNK signaling pathway, thereby providing effective treatment for IS (Fig. 15C).Similarly, Ma et al. created a ternary nanozyme (CUR/ZnCe-LRH) by coupling curcumin (CUR) with ZnCe layered rare earth hydroxide [143]. This nanozyme exhibited SOD, CAT activity, and ·OH capture ability. CUR acted as an enzyme activator, enhancing the activity of ZnCe-LRH. This approach not only reduced oxidative damage and apoptosis in brain neurons but also achieved efficient ROS scavenging, resulting in a significant improvement in IS control (Fig. 15D). In another study, Liu et al. synthesized Co–Fe3O4 nanozymes by doping Co into Fe3O4 [144]. These nanozymes effectively scavenged H2O2 and oxygen free radicals both in vitro and in vivo, providing neuroprotection against ischemic stroke. In models of permanent thromboembolic stroke and transient MCAO, Co–Fe3O4 nanozymes specifically accumulated at the infarction edge and were endocytosed by neurons, astrocytes, microglia, and endothelial cells. Despite concerns that Co–Fe3O4 might induce neuroinflammatory responses, both short-term and long-term studies showed no signs of inflammatory response or peripheral organ toxicity in healthy mice.

Huntington's disease (HD) is an incurable neurodegenerative disorder characterized by progressive neurological loss, influenced by epigenetic, oxidative stress, metabolic, and nutritional factors. The pathogenesis of HD is often linked to the amplification of CAG trinucleotide repeats in the HTT gene, which encodes the Huntington protein. In carriers of the mutant gene, the Huntington protein contains an unusually long polyglutamine sequence, which confers a toxic gain of function, making the protein prone to cleavage and leading to neuronal dysfunction and death [195]. However, targeted inhibition of Huntington protein aggregation has shown limited success in treating HD. Recent studies suggest that nanozymes with antioxidant properties could provide a promising strategy for HD treatment by mitigating oxidative stress. Aniruddha Adhikari et al. developed citric acid-functionalized manganese-based nanozymes (C–Mn3O4) with excellent biocompatibility. These nanozymes effectively mimic GPx enzyme activity in physiological conditions [145]. In a clinical animal model of HD, C–Mn3O4 NPs demonstrated high specificity for H2O2 and, when coupled with glutathione reductase (GR), successfully removed H2O2 and maintained cellular redox balance, thus reducing oxidative damage. The study using 3-nitropropionic acid (3-NPA)-induced mice confirmed that C–Mn3O4 nanozymes can cross the BBB and retain their enzymatic activity in brain cells, offering a novel approach to treating HD neurodegenerative diseases (Fig. 16 A).Zhang et al. synthesized MnFe2O4 nanozymes with good biocompatibility and tested them in mouse neuroblastoma cells (Neuro 2A) [146]. These nanozymes uniquely accelerated the removal of mutant Huntington protein with 74 glutamine repeats [Htt (Q74)]. The MnFe2O4 nanozymes facilitated the efficient degradation of Htt (Q74), bypassing autophagy pathways. These findings reveal a new biological function of MnFe2O4 nanozymes and suggest potential strategies for treating HD and other neurodegenerative diseases using bioactive nanomaterials (Fig. 16B).

Urinary tract infections (UTIs) are among the most common bacterial infections and are primarily caused by uropathogenic Escherichia coli (UPEC). These urinary tract pathogens possess unique characteristics, such as the production of adhesins, siderophores, and toxins, which enable them to adhere to and invade the urinary tract. They can spread between individuals through human-to-human contact and potentially via food or water [196]. Zhang et al. reported a bioinspired dextran (DEX)-modified cerium dioxide (DEC) nanozyme designed for UTI treatment. The DEX modification enhances the dispersion of ultra-small yttrium dioxide and provides multivalent binding sites that interact with the FimH protein, thereby mimicking the function of UMOD [147]. The DEC nanozymes can be metabolized by the kidneys, leading to reduced bacterial content in the urinary tract, as well as decreased inflammation and tissue damage. DEC nanozymes exhibited excellent antioxidant capacity against H2O2, lipopolysaccharide (LPS), and FimH-driven oxidative stress. In a mouse model, DEC nanozymes effectively treated acute urinary tract infections, recurrent infections, and catheter-related UTIs (Fig. 16C). This dual approach not only underscores the potential of nanozymes in treating UTIs but also demonstrates their applicability to other FimH-induced infections in the lungs and intestines, representing a significant advancement in nanozyme-based clinical applications. Tarricone et al. reported a 5 nm platinum nanozyme (Pt), which has POD, CAT, SOD and OX enzyme activities, and plays different catalytic mechanisms in different biological environments, and has the therapeutic potential to scavenge ROS in different cell models of neurovascular unit (NVU) [197]. Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is a high incidence of eye disease in the elderly, mainly due to the strong aerobic metabolism of rod cells and cone cells. Cupini et al. used platinum-based nanozymes to mimic natural POD, CAT and SOD with intrinsic multi-enzyme activity [198]. The effect of light damage in dark-fed albino rats was tested by in vivo electroretinogram (ERG), in vitro retinal morphology and electrophysiology. The results showed that Pt NPs could maintain retinal ERG activity and retinal ganglion cell response, reduce degenerative inflammatory response, making it a potential strategy for the treatment of AMD.

With advancements in understanding disease mechanisms and nanotechnology, the application prospects of nanozymes in the biomedical field are expanding significantly. Antioxidant nanozymes, particularly those with SOD, CAT, and POD-like activities, have emerged as powerful tools for treating neurological and inflammatory diseases due to their ability to scavenge free radicals and regulate oxidative stress. This article reviews recent developments in antioxidant nanozymes and their applications in addressing oxidative stress-related diseases, including neurological conditions such as Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease, and inflammatory diseases like inflammatory enteritis, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and ischemic stroke. By mimicking natural enzyme activities, these nanozymes effectively neutralize excess free radicals and slow disease progression. Preclinical studies have demonstrated that nanozymes can successfully inhibit disease progression by reducing free radical levels and impacting other pathological markers.

However, despite significant progress, the clinical translation of nanozymes still faces several challenges.
(1)
Catalytic mechanism and performance optimization: The molecular mechanism of the catalytic activity and action pathway of antioxidant nanozymes is not fully understood. It is particularly important to study the molecular mechanism of the catalytic activity and action pathway of antioxidant nanozymes. This not only helps to reveal its specific mode of action in vivo, but also provides a theoretical basis for the design of more efficient antioxidant nanozymes. Through a comprehensive understanding of its working mechanism, more targeted improvement strategies can be developed to broaden its application in medicine, environmental science and other fields.
(2)
Biosafety and compatibility: Ensuring the biosafety of nanozymes is crucial. Their potential intrinsic toxicity may pose a threat to human health. Strict safety assessment is required before clinical application to ensure its biocompatibility and long-term stability. In the process of safety assessment, it is necessary to detect the behavior of nanozymes in different physiological environments, including their degradation rate, interaction with biomolecules and possible immune responses. Further promote its application in the diagnosis, treatment and biosensing of antioxidant diseases to ensure that it benefits human beings while minimizing potential risks.
(3)
Feasibility of clinical application: The clinical application of nanozymes must address challenges related to production scale, cost-effectiveness, long-term stability and clinical safety. Actively explore new synthetic methods and optimize existing processes to achieve large-scale production of nanozymes and reduce costs. At the same time, the surface modification and stability of nanozymes were studied to improve their long-term stability in different physiological environments. Strict toxicological evaluation and clinical trials are being carried out to ensure the safety and effectiveness of nanozymes in practical applications. In addition, by designing an intelligent drug delivery system, precise delivery of nanozymes can be achieved, reducing side effects and improving therapeutic effects. With the continuous breakthrough of these key technologies, the application prospect of nanozymes in clinical treatment will be broader, which is expected to bring revolutionary changes to the diagnosis and treatment of antioxidant diseases.
(4)
Environmental stability and persistence: The stability and persistence of nanozymes under different environmental conditions is a key issue. Temperature, pH, ionic strength and other factors may affect its activity and function. Therefore, in-depth study of the effects of these factors on the properties of nanozymes is crucial to expand their applications. This requires experiments in a variety of simulated environments to accurately grasp the behavior changes of nanozymes, thereby optimizing their use conditions.
(5)
Multidisciplinary research: Promoting the further development of nanozyme technology requires close cooperation of multiple disciplines, including materials science, biochemistry, medical engineering, etc. This interdisciplinary research method can provide new ideas and methods for the design, optimization and application of nanozymes, so as to accelerate its application in biomedicine, environmental monitoring and other fields.

In summary, antioxidant nanozymes hold significant promise for treating ROS-related diseases. To achieve widespread application, future research must focus on gaining a deeper understanding of their mechanisms of action, improving biosafety and clinical applicability, and developing effective delivery systems. With these advancements, nanozymes have the potential to make a substantial impact on disease treatment.
